description: The rigorous study of the real number system, sequences, series, continuity, differentiation, and integration using ε-δ methods. Provides the theoretical foundation for calculus and functional analysis.

concepts.name: Completeness of ℝ
concepts.description: Every non-empty subset of ℝ bounded above has a supremum (least upper bound). Equivalently: every Cauchy sequence in ℝ converges. This is what distinguishes ℝ from ℚ and is the basis of all convergence arguments.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.1; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.1

concepts.name: Supremum and Infimum
concepts.description: sup(S) = least upper bound of S; inf(S) = greatest lower bound. For bounded sets: sup/inf always exist in ℝ (completeness). sup is attained iff it is a maximum; similarly for inf.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §1.7–1.10

concepts.name: Archimedean Property
concepts.description: For any real x, there exists a natural number n with n>x. Equivalently: the naturals are unbounded in ℝ. Corollary: ℚ is dense in ℝ (between any two reals there is a rational).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §1.20

concepts.name: Convergence of Sequences
concepts.description: Rigorous definition: aₙ→L iff ∀ε>0, ∃N: n>N ⟹ |aₙ−L|<ε. Sequence properties: convergent ⟹ bounded; limit laws; Bolzano-Weierstrass (every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.3; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Cauchy Sequence
concepts.description: A sequence where terms become arbitrarily close: ∀ε>0, ∃N: m,n>N ⟹ |aₘ−aₙ|<ε. In ℝ: Cauchy ⟺ convergent (completeness). Foundation for defining completeness in metric spaces.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §3.11

concepts.name: Series (Rigorous)
concepts.description: Rigorous treatment: ∑aₙ converges iff partial sums form a Cauchy sequence. Absolute convergence (∑|aₙ|<∞) implies convergence and rearrangement invariance. Conditional convergence is rearrangement-sensitive (Riemann rearrangement theorem).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.3; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.2

concepts.name: Absolute Convergence
concepts.description: ∑aₙ absolutely converges iff ∑|aₙ| converges. Implies convergence of ∑aₙ. Absolutely convergent series may be rearranged to any order without changing the sum. Conditionally convergent series can be rearranged to any value.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §3.47

concepts.name: Metric Space
concepts.description: A set X with a metric d:X×X→ℝ≥0 satisfying: d(x,y)=0⟺x=y, symmetry, triangle inequality. Examples: ℝⁿ (Euclidean), C([a,b]) (sup metric), discrete metric. Generalizes real analysis to abstract spaces.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.2; Munkres, Topology, Ch.2

concepts.name: Open and Closed Sets
concepts.description: Open ball: B(x,r)={y:d(x,y)<r}. Open set: every point is interior (surrounded by an open ball inside the set). Closed set: complement is open, equivalently contains all its limit points. Closure: smallest closed set containing S.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §2.18–2.26

concepts.name: Completeness of Metric Spaces
concepts.description: A metric space is complete iff every Cauchy sequence converges. ℝⁿ is complete; ℚ is not. Completion: every metric space embeds densely into a complete space. The Baire category theorem holds in complete metric spaces.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §3.11

concepts.name: Compactness
concepts.description: A set K is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover. Heine-Borel (in ℝⁿ): compact ⟺ closed and bounded. Sequential compactness: every sequence has a convergent subsequence (equivalent in metric spaces).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §2.31; Munkres, Topology, Ch.3

concepts.name: Connectedness
concepts.description: A set E is connected if it cannot be written as the union of two non-empty separated sets. Intervals in ℝ are the only connected subsets. Preserved under continuous maps. Intermediate Value Theorem: consequence of connectedness.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §2.45

concepts.name: Continuity (ε-δ)
concepts.description: f is continuous at a iff ∀ε>0, ∃δ>0: |x−a|<δ ⟹ |f(x)−f(a)|<ε. Equivalently: sequences xₙ→a ⟹ f(xₙ)→f(a). In metric spaces: preimages of open sets are open.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.4; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.4

concepts.name: Uniform Continuity
concepts.description: f is uniformly continuous on E iff ∀ε>0, ∃δ>0: |x−y|<δ ⟹ |f(x)−f(y)|<ε (δ depends only on ε, not on x). Every continuous function on a compact set is uniformly continuous (Heine-Cantor theorem).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §4.18

concepts.name: Lipschitz Continuity
concepts.description: |f(x)−f(y)| ≤ L|x−y| for some constant L (Lipschitz constant). Lipschitz ⟹ uniformly continuous ⟹ continuous. Important in ODE existence-uniqueness (Picard-Lindelöf) and optimization.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §4; Royden, Real Analysis, Ch.5

concepts.name: Extreme Value Theorem
concepts.description: A continuous function on a compact (closed, bounded in ℝⁿ) set attains its maximum and minimum. Proof: image of compact set under continuous map is compact; compact sets in ℝ have a max and min.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §4.16

concepts.name: Intermediate Value Theorem (Rigorous)
concepts.description: If f:[a,b]→ℝ is continuous and f(a)<c<f(b), then ∃x∈(a,b) with f(x)=c. Proof: connected image of connected set is connected; connected subsets of ℝ are intervals.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §4.23; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, §4.5

concepts.name: Differentiability (Rigorous)
concepts.description: f′(a) = lim_{h→0} [f(a+h)−f(a)]/h. Differentiable ⟹ continuous; converse fails (|x| at 0). In ℝⁿ: total derivative Df(a) is a linear map; differentiable implies all partials exist but not conversely.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.5

concepts.name: Mean Value Theorem (Rigorous)
concepts.description: If f:[a,b]→ℝ is continuous, differentiable on (a,b), then ∃c∈(a,b) with f′(c)=(f(b)−f(a))/(b−a). Proved via Rolle's theorem. Fundamental tool: f′=0 on interval ⟹ f constant.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §5.10

concepts.name: Taylor's Theorem (Rigorous)
concepts.description: f(x) = ∑_{k=0}^n f^{(k)}(a)/k! (x−a)^k + R_n(x) where the Lagrange remainder R_n(x) = f^{(n+1)}(c)/(n+1)! (x−a)^{n+1} for some c between a and x. Gives quantitative error bounds for polynomial approximations.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §5.15

concepts.name: Riemann Integral (Rigorous)
concepts.description: Partition P of [a,b]; upper/lower Darboux sums U(f,P) and L(f,P). f is Riemann integrable iff inf U(f,P) = sup L(f,P). Riemann integrable functions are bounded; continuous functions are integrable.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.6; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.7

concepts.name: Riemann Integrability
concepts.description: f is Riemann integrable on [a,b] iff f is bounded and its set of discontinuities has Lebesgue measure zero. Continuous functions and monotone functions are integrable. A function with infinitely many discontinuities can still be integrable.
references: Royden, Real Analysis, Ch.4

concepts.name: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Rigorous)
concepts.description: Part 1: If f integrable and F(x)=∫_a^x f, then F is Lipschitz; if f continuous at x₀ then F′(x₀)=f(x₀). Part 2: If F is an antiderivative of f, then ∫_a^b f = F(b)−F(a). Proof uses the MVT and Riemann sum properties.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §6.20; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.7

concepts.name: Uniform Convergence
concepts.description: fₙ→f uniformly on E iff ∀ε>0, ∃N: n>N ⟹ sup_{x∈E}|fₙ(x)−f(x)|<ε. Uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous; integrates termwise; differentiates termwise if derivatives converge uniformly.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, Ch.7; Abbot, Understanding Analysis, Ch.6

concepts.name: Pointwise Convergence
concepts.description: fₙ→f pointwise on E iff ∀x∈E, ∀ε>0, ∃N(x,ε): n>N ⟹ |fₙ(x)−f(x)|<ε. Weaker than uniform convergence; the limit of continuous functions need not be continuous. Dini's theorem: monotone pointwise limit on compact set is uniform.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §7.1

concepts.name: Weierstrass Approximation Theorem
concepts.description: Every continuous function on [a,b] can be uniformly approximated by polynomials. Proved by Bernstein polynomials. Stone-Weierstrass generalizes to compact Hausdorff spaces with algebras separating points.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §7.26; Rudin, Real and Complex Analysis, Ch.5

concepts.name: Banach Fixed-Point Theorem
concepts.description: A contraction mapping T on a complete metric space (|d(Tx,Ty)| ≤ k·d(x,y), k<1) has a unique fixed point, attained in the limit of iterated applications T^n(x₀). Used to prove ODE existence-uniqueness (Picard iteration).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §9.23 (implicit function); Coddington & Levinson, Ch.1

concepts.name: Baire Category Theorem
concepts.description: A complete metric space cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere dense sets. Equivalently: a countable intersection of dense open sets is dense. Key consequence: functions with everywhere-defined derivative cannot be continuous but nowhere differentiable (wait — no: Baire implies such functions exist and form a residual set).
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §5.6; Royden, Real Analysis, Ch.7

concepts.name: Implicit Function Theorem
concepts.description: If F:ℝⁿ×ℝᵐ→ℝᵐ is C¹ and F(a,b)=0 with ∂F/∂y invertible at (a,b), then locally y is a C¹ function of x with y(a)=b. Used to solve equations implicitly near known solutions.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §9.28; Munkres, Analysis on Manifolds

concepts.name: Inverse Function Theorem
concepts.description: If f:ℝⁿ→ℝⁿ is C¹ and Df(a) is invertible, then f is locally invertible near a with C¹ inverse satisfying D(f⁻¹)(f(a)) = [Df(a)]⁻¹. Proved using the Banach fixed-point theorem.
references: Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis, §9.24
